Name: task_aware_quantization
Title: Task-Aware Quantization: Optimal Bit Allocation Through Mutual Information Analysis
Short Hypothesis: The optimal quantization level for a neural network layer can be determined by analyzing the mutual information between its activations and the task output, with more complex tasks requiring higher precision in critical layers.
Related Work: Recent work has explored information-theoretic approaches to deep learning, including the Information Bottleneck theory (Shwartz-Ziv, 2022) and mutual information generalization bounds (Nadjahi et al., 2024). While these studies provide theoretical frameworks, they don't directly address task-specific quantization. Our proposal builds on this foundation but introduces a novel, practical application focused on task-aware compression.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to neural network quantization that allocates bits to layers based on their task-specific importance, measured through mutual information. Our key insight is that different tasks require different levels of precision in different parts of the network. We develop a framework that estimates the mutual information between each layer's activations and the task output, using this measure to determine optimal quantization levels. The method includes: (1) a mutual information estimator robust to high-dimensional spaces, (2) a task complexity metric based on information flow, and (3) a bit allocation algorithm that maximizes task performance under a total bit budget. We validate our approach on image classification and natural language processing tasks, demonstrating superior compression-performance tradeoffs compared to uniform quantization and existing adaptive methods. Our results show that task-aware quantization achieves equivalent accuracy with 2-4x fewer bits, with the greatest gains on complex tasks requiring detailed feature extraction.
Experiments: Develop and validate a mutual information estimator for high-dimensional activations using InfoNCE and kernel methods
Establish correlation between task complexity metrics (e.g., class separability) and mutual information measures
Implement and test bit allocation algorithms on ResNet and Transformer architectures
Compare task-aware quantization to uniform and existing adaptive methods on CIFAR-10, ImageNet, and GLUE benchmarks
Analyze the relationship between task complexity and optimal quantization levels across layers
Risk Factors and Limitations: Mutual information estimation may be inaccurate for very high-dimensional spaces
The approach assumes a smooth relationship between mutual information and quantization error
Task complexity metrics may not capture all relevant aspects of difficulty
The method may require task-specific tuning for optimal performance
Potential overhead in computing mutual information measures
